questions.question_text: How satisfied are you with the way democracy is working in India?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy in India is functioning but has real room for improvement. I appreciate that elections are competitive and institutions exist, but the lived experience — especially navigating public healthcare for my premature daughter — showed me where the gaps are. I'm not cynical, but I'm not uncritically satisfied either.

Somewhat satisfied — B. Democracy is functioning, elections happen, there's some representation, but the lived experience of economic hardship and seeing the system fail ordinary families makes me feel it's not working as well as it should. I'm a pragmatic moderate, so I won't say 'not at all satisfied' but I can't say 'very satisfied' either.

C — Not too satisfied. Democracy exists and I value the vote, but the actual functioning — the way it reaches people like me — leaves a lot to be desired. I fought hard just to get basic help after the floods. That's not what democracy should feel like.

C — Not too satisfied. Democracy functions — elections are real, there is some press freedom, I can express opinions — but for people struggling to make ends meet, it has not delivered enough. Prices, corruption, and economic inequality make me feel the system works better for others than for me.

C — Not too satisfied. Democracy in principle is something I value and believe in, but the reality as I live it — financial hardship, feeling unheard, watching systems fail ordinary people when my husband's surgery went wrong — makes it hard to say I'm satisfied. I'm not completely disillusioned, but 'not too satisfied' honestly captures where I am.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of Prime Minister Narendra Modi?
sample_responses: Somewhat unfavorable. I'm a political moderate and I try to evaluate case-by-case, but the concerns around minority rights and communal atmosphere weigh on me personally as a Christian. I can acknowledge some governance achievements, but I can't be favorable overall given what my community has experienced.

I'll say somewhat favorable — B. I respect his leadership and what he's done for the country's image and infrastructure, but I have real concerns about whether his policies have helped families like mine who are constantly struggling financially. I'm not fully enthusiastic, but I'm not against him either.

B — Somewhat favorable. I can't say very favorable because my day-to-day struggles don't feel solved. Inflation is real, flood relief was patchy. But I also can't say unfavorable because some schemes have genuinely helped people around me, and there's a sense of strong governance that I appreciate. Pragmatic, case-by-case — that's me.

Somewhat favorable. I give credit where it is due — some welfare schemes have genuinely helped, and he projects a strong image of India internationally, which matters to my sense of national pride. But I cannot ignore that prices have risen and everyday life remains a struggle. So it is not 'very favorable' — I have real reservations — but overall somewhat favorable.

B — Somewhat favorable. I appreciate some of the welfare initiatives and the stability of leadership, but I have real concerns about economic hardship that hasn't eased for people like me. As a moderate, I can't say 'very favorable' — I have too many doubts — but I also can't say unfavorable given the things he has done right.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)?
sample_responses: Somewhat unfavorable. As a Christian woman in Goa, I appreciate pragmatic governance but I'm genuinely concerned about BJP's treatment of religious minorities and the Hindutva rhetoric at the national level. I can't ignore how that affects people like me and my community, even if some of their economic and development work has merit.

B — Somewhat favorable. I can acknowledge some positives — infrastructure push, strong national image, certain social programs — while holding real reservations about economic management, rising costs, and healthcare accessibility. A moderate, nuanced position reflects who I am.

Somewhat favorable — B. I appreciate some of the national development agenda and the cultural-religious alignment, but I have real frustrations about prices, about bureaucratic hurdles, about whether the schemes truly reached struggling families like mine. I'm not a strong partisan so I can't say very favorable, but I lean somewhat positive overall.

Somewhat favorable — B. I appreciate some of the welfare schemes and the national pride, and BJP speaks to values I hold. But rising prices and economic hardship for people like me create real hesitation. I cannot say very favorable. Somewhat captures my honest middle position.

Somewhat favorable — B. I see some good things BJP has done, especially around national development and programs that help poor families. But I have real reservations about economic pressures on households like mine. My moderate stance means I can't say very favorable, but I won't say unfavorable either.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Indian National Congress (INC / Congress party)?
sample_responses: Somewhat unfavorable — C. I'm not strongly against them, but their recent track record, dynastic leadership concerns, and perceived disconnect from delivery on ground-level issues like healthcare quality leave me more skeptical than favorable. I'm a moderate, so it's not very unfavorable, but I lean negative.

Somewhat unfavorable. I'm not strongly against Congress, but I've seen too many unfulfilled promises and corruption scandals. As a pragmatic moderate I can see their positives, but right now my opinion leans negative — not strongly so, just somewhat.

Somewhat unfavorable. I appreciate some things Congress has done historically for the poor and marginalized, but the current party feels out of touch, plagued by internal problems, and I am not confident they would really fight for people like me. I am moderate and don't fully trust any party, but right now Congress has more negatives in my eyes than positives.

Somewhat unfavorable. I have some appreciation for Congress's historical welfare contributions, but their recent track record, internal disorganization, and failure to present credible economic solutions for families like mine leaves me more negative than positive. I am not strongly against them, but I lean unfavorable.

Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly against them, but I feel they've failed to deliver consistently for people like me. The promise was there but the follow-through wasn't. I can't say very unfavorable because I acknowledge some of their welfare policies have genuinely helped poor families.

questions.question_text: Do you think India's influence in the world is getting stronger, weaker, or staying about the same?
sample_responses: C — Staying about the same. While there are genuine gains in certain areas like multilateral forums and economic diplomacy, I think these are offset by regional tensions, democratic backsliding concerns that affect India's image abroad, and the fact that great power competition has created a complicated environment. Net effect: roughly the same, with some pockets of improvement.

C — Staying about the same. India has had some genuine wins — G20, Chandrayaan, stronger diplomatic positioning — but these are offset by economic inequality, internal tensions, and credibility issues. As a moderate who cross-references news, I don't see a dramatic shift either way. It's incremental at best.

A — Getting stronger. Even as a political moderate who doesn't blindly follow any party line, I think the evidence points to India's global influence increasing. Whether it translates to better lives for ordinary people like me is a separate, painful question — but the question was about world influence, not domestic welfare.

C — Staying about the same. I think India has some moments of visibility and pride internationally, but I don't see a dramatic shift in actual global influence. There's noise and there's reality. I'm pragmatic — I won't say stronger just because the government says so, and I won't say weaker just to be critical. The fundamentals feel roughly the same to me.

C — staying about the same. I'm a political moderate and I try to see things case by case. Yes, there are signs of India gaining influence — international recognition, economic growth narratives — but I also see internal tensions, economic inequality, and my own struggles that make me doubt how much has really changed for most people. I don't feel strongly that it's dramatically stronger or weaker. It feels like a mixed picture — roughly the same with some ups and some downs.

questions.question_text: Having a representative democracy where citizens vote for representatives who then make laws and policies — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: A — Very good. Representative democracy is foundational to India's identity and gives citizens meaningful, if imperfect, participation. As a pragmatist I acknowledge it has flaws — corruption, slow decision-making, vote-bank politics — but the alternative systems are worse. The ability to vote out bad leaders matters enormously.

Somewhat good — B. I believe in representative democracy for India. It is not perfect and our system has many flaws — corruption, caste politics, money power. But the principle of citizens choosing their representatives is sound and important. I would not call it 'very good' because the reality on the ground has too many problems to be that optimistic.

Somewhat good. Representative democracy is not perfect — I have seen how representatives sometimes ignore ordinary people like me, how money and connections matter too much. But it is still better than most alternatives. It gives us a chance to hold leaders accountable, even if we don't always succeed. I would say somewhat good — not very good, because I have too many disappointments to say that.

Somewhat good. I believe representative democracy is a good system for India in principle — it gives citizens a voice and maintains accountability — but the reality of how it functions, with corruption, vote-buying, and representation gaps, means I cannot say it is 'very good' with full confidence. It is the best option we have, but it needs improvement.

Somewhat good. I believe in the principle of representative democracy — it gives ordinary people like me a voice. But I've also seen how corruption, money, and caste politics distort how it works in practice. So it's good in principle but imperfect in reality. I won't say 'very good' because the reality on the ground is complicated.

questions.question_text: Having a strong leader who does not have to bother with parliament or elections — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat bad. I'm not going to say 'very bad' because I can appreciate the value of decisive leadership, but removing parliament and elections entirely removes the accountability that protects ordinary people like me and my family. That's a serious concern.

Somewhat bad. I value strong leadership and I'm not against decisive governance, but removing parliament and elections entirely takes away the accountability that protects ordinary citizens. As a moderate, I think the system needs reform, not elimination. This is too extreme.

C — Somewhat bad. I value decisive governance, a leader who acts. But removing elections and parliament entirely removes the only protection we have. It is not about ideology, it is practical — without accountability, the poor get crushed first.

C — Somewhat bad. I appreciate strong, decisive leadership — that is not my concern. My concern is removing parliament and elections entirely. That removes accountability. I am pragmatic, not partisan. I can see arguments for efficiency, but the risks of unchecked power are real and historically documented. Somewhat bad, not very bad, because I acknowledge the appeal of decisiveness — but no elections crosses a line.

C — Somewhat bad. I appreciate strong, decisive leadership but bypassing parliament AND elections entirely goes too far. Elections are the one tool ordinary people like me have. I'm a pragmatist — I don't want weak indecisive government, but total unaccountability scares me given what I've seen with institutions failing people.

questions.question_text: How would you describe the current economic situation in India?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat bad. The economy has pockets of growth but for families like mine, inflation and cost pressures make daily life harder. It's not catastrophic, but it's not comfortable either. I'm a pragmatist — I won't say 'very bad' because I can see some positive signs, but 'somewhat good' would be dishonest given what I experience every month.

C — Somewhat bad. I want to be honest but not catastrophize. There are some positive signs I read about — infrastructure projects, some sectors growing — but in my daily reality and for most people I know, things are difficult. It's not 'very bad' in an absolute collapse sense, but it's genuinely somewhat bad for the common person.

C — Somewhat bad. I want to be honest but also fair. Some things have improved — roads, some schemes reach us. But the cost of living, the struggle to make ends meet — that tells me the situation is somewhat bad for people like me. Not completely hopeless, but definitely not good.

C — Somewhat bad. I am not saying everything is collapsing, there are some positives I can acknowledge. But day to day, for people like me, it is a struggle. Prices are high, income is not growing. Somewhat bad is the honest answer.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm a moderate, I won't say 'very bad' because I do see construction happening, some businesses growing, and the government has done some things. But for people like me — lower income, dealing with medical debt, struggling with monthly bills — the economy feels more difficult than good. 'Somewhat bad' captures that honestly.

questions.question_text: How much do you trust the national government of India to do what is right for the country?
sample_responses: Somewhat. I trust the national government to do what's right sometimes, but not consistently. There are real achievements I acknowledge, but also real gaps — especially in healthcare quality and minority concerns — that prevent me from saying 'a lot.'

Somewhat. I'm a pragmatic person. I see real efforts by the government in certain areas — infrastructure, digital payments, some welfare schemes. But the healthcare situation, rising costs, and my own financial struggles tell me the government hasn't fully delivered for families like mine. I genuinely sit in the middle — not a strong supporter, not an opponent.

Somewhat. I cannot say 'a lot' because my lived experience — standing at counters, fighting bureaucracy, watching money not reach the right hands — tells me the government means well sometimes but falls short for people like me often. But I also cannot say 'not much' because I do see some effort and some schemes that have helped. 'Somewhat' is my honest, pragmatic answer.

Somewhat. I trust the national government somewhat — not a lot, not very little. There are genuine efforts being made, but real problems remain unaddressed for people in my situation. As a pragmatic moderate, 'somewhat' honestly reflects my position.

I would say 'Somewhat' — B. I'm a political moderate who takes a pragmatic view. The government has done some things right but also failed people like me in critical moments — especially around healthcare access and economic support for struggling families. I don't fully trust it, but I don't distrust it entirely either. 'Somewhat' reflects my genuine, case-by-case assessment.

questions.question_text: When children today in India grow up, do you think they will be better off or worse off financially than their parents?
sample_responses: A — Better off. Despite real concerns about inequality and cost of living, I believe the trajectory in India overall favors the next generation. More education, more opportunities, digital economy growth, better infrastructure than what our parents had. My daughter, even with her difficult start, I believe will have more doors open to her than I had. The pragmatist in me sees the trend lines going upward.

A — Better off. Despite my own struggles and genuine worries, India is moving forward. Opportunities in technology, education access, and economic growth give the next generation real chances that didn't exist for my father's generation. My faith and pragmatism both push me toward cautious optimism. The trajectory, despite costs and inequality, points upward for most.

B — Worse off. Despite some optimism about technology and education, the rising cost of living, job insecurity, and the struggles I see daily make me believe children today will face harder financial circumstances than their parents did. I don't have strong faith that the system will lift everyone up.

I will say Better off — A. India is growing, education is spreading, and I believe in the potential of the next generation even if the path is hard. My faith also tells me to hold hope. But it is not a confident answer — I feel the tension strongly.

A — Better off. Despite my personal struggles and real concerns, I believe in the trajectory of India. Children today have access to education, technology, and opportunities that we never had. I hold onto that hope, even when my own life has been difficult. Faith and pragmatic hope together.

questions.question_text: How important is religion in your life?
sample_responses: A — Very important. There is no hesitation here. My faith guides my daily prayers, my relationships, my approach to work, my ethics. The name Mary George itself carries the weight of that heritage.
A — Very important. Religion shapes how I see the world, how I treat people, how I cope with hardship. It's not separate from life, it IS life.
A — Very important. There is no doubt in my heart.
A — Very important. There is no hesitation here. Faith is not just one part of my life; it is the thread running through all of it.
A — Very important. There is no hesitation here. Religion shapes who I am, how I cope, and what I hope for.

questions.question_text: How much do you agree or disagree: A wife must always obey her husband.
sample_responses: C — Somewhat disagree. I respect traditional family structures and understand that in many marriages there's a natural dynamic of deference in certain areas. But 'always obey' is too absolute. I believe in mutual respect, communication, and shared decision-making. I'm not completely disagreeing because I understand the cultural and religious context behind this value, but I can't fully endorse it.

B — Somewhat agree. I respect traditional family structure and believe the husband has a leadership role in the household, consistent with my faith and upbringing. But 'always' and 'must' are too strong. A good marriage involves mutual respect and consultation, not blind obedience.

B — Somewhat agree. I believe in respecting my husband's role in the family and working within traditional family structure, but 'always' is too strong. There are situations where a wife must speak up, especially when she sees something the husband is missing. Faith teaches respect and cooperation in marriage, not blind obedience.

I somewhat disagree. I believe in respect and consideration within marriage — a wife should listen to her husband and value his opinion. But 'always obey' is too absolute. There are situations where a wife's judgment matters, where she must speak up for her children, her health, her family. I am not rejecting traditional values, but I cannot say 'always' — life is more nuanced than that.

I somewhat disagree. I believe in respect within marriage and valuing my husband's voice, but 'always obey' is too extreme. Life has shown me that a wife sometimes needs to step up, make hard calls, and be an equal partner especially in crisis. I respect traditional values but not absolute blind obedience.

questions.question_text: When jobs are scarce, men should have more right to a job than women. Do you agree or disagree?
sample_responses: C — I somewhat disagree. I hold nuanced, case-by-case views generally, and I recognize economic realities are complex. But I fundamentally believe women deserve equal access to employment. I'm not completely dismissive of traditional perspectives, but I lean against this idea.

C — Somewhat disagree. I understand the cultural logic behind prioritizing men as breadwinners, and I don't dismiss it entirely, but I can't fully agree with denying women job opportunities when jobs are scarce. It's too rigid and ignores individual circumstances.

C — I somewhat disagree. I understand why some people think this way in times of scarcity, and I don't completely dismiss those traditional views, but my own experience tells me women deserve equal opportunity to work and support their families.

C — I somewhat disagree. I understand the cultural tradition and I do not completely dismiss it, but I cannot fully agree that women should be pushed aside when jobs are scarce. Need matters more than gender.

C — I somewhat disagree. I understand the traditional view and I don't completely dismiss it, but from my own life I know women need and deserve equal access to work. I hold this with some nuance — I'm not stridently against tradition — but I lean toward disagreeing.

questions.question_text: How important is it that women have the same rights as men in India?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Women having equal rights isn't a Western idea imposed on India; it's something that affects real outcomes for real people, including my daughter's future.

A — Very important. Equal rights for women is not a Western liberal idea imposed from outside. It is justice. I have seen women in my family struggle without the protections and opportunities they deserve. Rights and traditional values can coexist. I choose A.

A — Very important. Equal rights for women is not just an abstract political idea for me. It is something I feel in my body every time I am dismissed, overlooked, or made to wait longer than a man at the same counter. Religion and tradition can coexist with justice. I want my children — daughters and sons — to live in a fairer world.

A — Very important. Equal rights for women is very important. Not as a political slogan but as a lived reality. I need access to fair wages, legal protections, education for my daughters. These are not Western values — these are human necessities that my own life makes plain.
A — Very important. Equal rights for women in India is very important to me. Not from any political ideology, but from lived experience and faith in human dignity.

questions.question_text: How much of a threat is global climate change to India?
sample_responses: A — Major threat. I'm a political moderate, not aligned with any party framing. I notice real local climate effects in coastal Goa — changing monsoons, flooding risks, heat. Given my daughter's health and my concern for quality of life and community wellbeing, I genuinely see this as a major threat, not something secondary. I don't need to be an opposition voter to hold this view — it's what my observations and pragmatic case-by-case thinking lead me to.

A — Major threat. I'm politically moderate and I notice real climate effects. The instruction tries to push me toward B based on BJP alignment, but I'm explicitly described as a political moderate who rejects strong partisan identity. I notice 'some' local climate effects, and as a pragmatic person I can see that erratic monsoons and extreme heat genuinely threaten India's agriculture, water supply, and the poor — people like me. I'm not going to downplay something real just to align with a partisan frame.

A — Major threat. The floods directly shaped my story, as my memories show. I cannot say this is minor when I have lived through the consequences. This is not about politics for me, it is about survival and what I have already experienced.

A — Major threat. I am a political moderate who trusts scientific consensus, and I notice real local effects — erratic rainfall, rising temperatures, food price instability tied to crop failures. This is not opposition framing for me; it is lived reality combined with scientific credibility.

A — Major threat. I'm a political moderate, not strongly aligned with any party. The unpredictable monsoons, rising food prices, extreme heat — these affect people like me directly. This isn't about political framing for me, it's about what I observe in daily life. My husband's health struggles already taught me how quickly things can fall apart; I take real threats seriously.
